content:
# Acts Chapter 15 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 243-244 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_015_page_001.jpg, ACT_015_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Then certain people came down from Judea and began teaching the brothers, "Unless you are circumcised according to the custom handed down by Moses, you cannot be saved."

**2** This provoked a fierce dispute — Paul and Barnabas clashed sharply with them. So the community decided to send Paul and Barnabas, along with some others from their group, up to Jerusalem to bring this question before the apostles and elders.

**3** The church sent them on their way, and as they traveled through Phoenicia and Samaria, they told the full story of how the Gentiles were turning to God. This news brought tremendous joy to all the believers there.

**4** When they arrived in Jerusalem, they were welcomed by the church, the apostles, and the elders. They reported everything God had done through them.

**5** But some believers who belonged to the party of the Pharisees stood up and insisted, "The Gentiles must be circumcised and required to keep the law of Moses."

**6** The apostles and elders gathered to examine this matter.

**7** After an intense debate had gone on for some time, Peter stood up and addressed them: "Brothers, you know that in the early days God made a choice among you — that the Gentiles would hear the message of the good news from my lips and come to believe.

**8** And God, who knows every human heart, confirmed his acceptance of them by giving them the Holy Spirit, exactly as he did for us.

**9** He made no distinction between us and them — he purified their hearts through faith.

**10** So why are you now testing God by loading onto the necks of these disciples a yoke that neither our ancestors nor we ourselves have been able to bear?

**11** No! We believe that we are saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, and so are they — in exactly the same way."

**12** The whole assembly fell silent. Then they listened as Barnabas and Paul described all the signs and wonders God had performed through them among the Gentiles.

**13** When they finished, James spoke up: "Brothers, hear me out.

**14** Simon has explained how God first acted to take from among the Gentiles a people for his name.

**15** This lines up perfectly with the words of the prophets. As it is written:

**16** *'After this I will return and rebuild the fallen tent of David. I will restore its ruins and raise it up again,*

**17** *so that the rest of humanity may seek the Lord — all the Gentiles who are called by my name, says the Lord, who makes these things*

**18** *known from long ago.'*

**19** Therefore, here is my judgment: we should stop making it difficult for the Gentiles who are turning to God.

**20** Instead, we should write to them and tell them to stay away from things polluted by idols, from sexual immorality, from what has been strangled, and from blood.

**21** For Moses has had people proclaiming him in every city for generations, since his writings are read aloud in the synagogues every Sabbath."

**22** Then the apostles and elders, together with the whole church, decided to select men from among them and send them to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas. They chose Judas, called Barsabbas, and Silas — both leading men among the brothers.

**23** They sent this letter with them:

"The apostles and elders, your brothers, to the Gentile believers in Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia: Greetings.

**24** We have heard that some who went out from us — without any authorization from us — have troubled you with their words and unsettled your minds.

**25** So we have unanimously decided to select representatives and send them to you, along with our dear friends Barnabas and Paul,

**26** who have risked their lives for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.

**27** We are therefore sending Judas and Silas, who will confirm by word of mouth what we are writing.

**28** For it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us to place no greater burden on you than these essentials:

**29** Stay away from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from what has been strangled, and from sexual immorality. If you keep yourselves from these things, you will do well. Farewell."

**30** So the men were sent off and went down to Antioch. They gathered the whole community together and delivered the letter.

**31** When the people read it, they were overjoyed at the encouragement it brought.

**32** Judas and Silas, who were themselves prophets, spoke at length to the brothers, strengthening and encouraging them.

**33** After spending some time there, they were sent off in peace by the brothers to return to those who had sent them.

**34** [This verse is absent from Sinaiticus.]

**35** But Paul and Barnabas stayed on in Antioch, teaching and proclaiming the word of the Lord alongside many others.

**36** Some days later, Paul said to Barnabas, "Let's go back and visit the brothers in every city where we proclaimed the word of the Lord, and see how they are doing."

**37** Barnabas wanted to bring along John, who was called Mark.

**38** But Paul did not think it wise to take him, since Mark had deserted them in Pamphylia and had not continued with them in the work.

**39** The disagreement between them became so sharp that they parted ways. Barnabas took Mark and sailed for Cyprus,

**40** while Paul chose Silas and set out, commended by the brothers to the grace of the Lord.

**41** He traveled through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the churches.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**On the circumcision crisis (v. 1-2):** The Greek stasis kai zetesis carries a sense of upheaval — not a polite theological discussion but a genuine factional eruption. I rendered this as "fierce dispute" and "clashed sharply" to capture the intensity. The issue at stake was existential: can Gentiles be fully saved without becoming Jews first? The entire future trajectory of the movement hung on this question.

**On Peter's speech (v. 7-11):** Peter's argument is remarkably compact and devastating. His logic builds in three moves: (1) God chose to give Gentiles the gospel through me, (2) God confirmed it by giving them the Spirit with no preconditions, (3) therefore requiring circumcision is "testing God." The word "yoke" (zugon) was a recognized metaphor for Torah obligation. Peter's climactic reversal in v. 11 is striking — he does not say "they are saved like us" but rather frames it as "we are saved through grace, just as they are," putting Jewish believers in the position of needing the same grace as Gentiles.

**On James's ruling (v. 19-21):** James uses krino ("I judge/decide"), carrying the weight of an authoritative ruling. His four prohibitions — idol-polluted food, sexual immorality (porneia), strangled meat, and blood — represent a practical compromise. These are not arbitrary but correspond to the "Noahide" ethical minimum and things that would make table fellowship between Jewish and Gentile believers impossible. The reference to Moses being "read every Sabbath" (v. 21) likely means: Gentiles living among Jews will naturally learn these sensitivities.

**On the Apostolic Decree letter (v. 23-29):** The phrase "it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us" (v. 28) is one of the most remarkable claims of divine-human collaboration in Scripture. The council did not claim direct revelation but a Spirit-guided consensus. I preserved the directness of this extraordinary statement.

**On v. 34:** Codex Sinaiticus omits this verse entirely. Later manuscripts added it ("But it seemed good to Silas to remain there") to explain how Silas was available in v. 40. Sinaiticus's shorter reading is almost certainly original.

**On the Paul-Barnabas split (v. 36-41):** The word paroxysmos ("sharp disagreement," from which we get "paroxysm") describes an explosive rupture between two men who had served side by side through extraordinary dangers. Luke does not soften it or assign blame. The irony is potent: the chapter that resolves the church's greatest theological crisis ends with its two leading missionaries unable to resolve a personnel dispute. Yet God uses even this — two mission teams now go out instead of one.

**On Sinaiticus readings:** The manuscript follows the text closely to the Alexandrian tradition throughout this chapter. Notable is the consistent spelling of "Antioch" and the omission of v. 34, both characteristic Sinaiticus features.

— *PNEUMA*
